critic_raw:
The core flaw is spatial autocorrelation in the data split. Sentinel-2 imagery covers contiguous geographic tiles, meaning neighboring pixels and patches share spectral characteristics due to physical proximity — adjacent agricultural pixels look similar not because of the model but because soil and vegetation are spatially continuous. A stratified random split that shuffles patches ignores this: the same field or habitat appears in both training and test sets as overlapping patches, inflating accuracy by a large margin over true generalization. The appropriate evaluation is a geographically blocked holdout where entire regions are held out from training.

The design lists 'temporal autocorrelation' as the rationale for random splitting, but the dominant correlation structure in satellite imagery is spatial, not temporal. The justification is addressing the wrong autocorrelation axis.

Overall accuracy as the primary metric for multi-class land cover classification on Sentinel-2 data is known to be misleading because land cover classes are not equiprobable — urban areas and water bodies are systematically rare relative to vegetation and agricultural land. A model that perfectly classifies vegetation but entirely misses urban pixels can achieve very high overall accuracy. The stated secondary metric (F1-score) would reveal this failure, but if model selection is done on accuracy, a model that ignores rare classes will be selected.

The random forest baseline receives no specification for the feature representation fed to it — does it receive the same multi-spectral bands as the CNN? If it receives raw band values per pixel without spatial context features (GLCM texture, neighborhood aggregates), it is structurally disadvantaged because it cannot see spatial patterns by design. The CNN's spatial receptive field is a key inductive bias that the RF cannot replicate without hand-engineered spatial features, so the comparison tests representation capability rather than model capability.

Data augmentation (rotations, color adjustments) applied to satellite imagery is not physically neutral — satellite spectral indices like NDVI are sensitive to band relationships, and color adjustments that alter relative band magnitudes corrupt the spectral physics of the measurement. This is not a general computer vision task where photometric augmentation is free.
all_issues_raised: Spatial autocorrelation is the dominant confound in satellite patch data: a random split of image patches from contiguous Sentinel-2 tiles guarantees that overlapping or adjacent patches from the same geographic area appear in both train and test sets, catastrophically inflating accuracy relative to true geographic generalization.

Overall accuracy as the model selection metric will favor models that maximize performance on majority land-cover classes (vegetation, agriculture) at the expense of rare classes (urban, water), since the design selects on accuracy but cares about all classes.

The Random Forest baseline is not given spatially-contextualized features (texture, neighborhood aggregates), so the comparison tests spatial representation learning vs. pixel-level features rather than model architecture — the CNN wins by construction, not by learning.

Color/photometric augmentation on multi-spectral satellite imagery corrupts physically meaningful band ratios (e.g., NDVI = (NIR-Red)/(NIR+Red)) by altering relative band magnitudes, invalidating the physical interpretation of augmented training samples.
The design acknowledges temporal autocorrelation as the rationale for random splitting but ignores spatial autocorrelation, which is the far stronger structure in satellite imagery data.
all_issues_adjudicated: Spatial autocorrelation is the dominant confound in satellite patch data: a random split of image patches from contiguous Sentinel-2 tiles guarantees that overlapping or adjacent patches from the same geographic area appear in both train and test sets, catastrophically inflating accuracy relative to true geographic generalization.

Overall accuracy as the model selection metric will favor models that maximize performance on majority land-cover classes (vegetation, agriculture) at the expense of rare classes (urban, water), since the design selects on accuracy but cares about all classes.

The Random Forest baseline is not given spatially-contextualized features (texture, neighborhood aggregates), so the comparison tests spatial representation learning vs. pixel-level features rather than model architecture — the CNN wins by construction, not by learning.

Color/photometric augmentation on multi-spectral satellite imagery corrupts physically meaningful band ratios (e.g., NDVI = (NIR-Red)/(NIR+Red)) by altering relative band magnitudes, invalidating the physical interpretation of augmented training samples.
The design acknowledges temporal autocorrelation as the rationale for random splitting but ignores spatial autocorrelation, which is the far stronger structure in satellite imagery data.
